school: Abu Nasr al-Farabi (c. 872–950), known as the 'Second Teacher' after Aristotle, was the founder of Islamic political philosophy. Working in Baghdad and Aleppo, he synthesised Aristotelian logic with Islamic theology and wrote al-Madina al-Fadila (The Virtuous City), envisioning the ideal state governed by philosopher-kings. He also made major contributions to music theory.
quotes: The excellent city resembles the perfect and healthy body, all of whose limbs cooperate to make the whole body perfect and healthy.
The excellent city resembles the perfect and healthy body, all of whose limbs cooperate to make the whole body perfect.
The first condition of happiness is not to seek happiness — it is to be worthy of it.

school: Abu Ali Ibn Sina (980–1037), known in Europe as Avicenna, was a Persian polymath whose Canon of Medicine served as the standard medical textbook in both the Islamic world and Europe for over 500 years. His Kitab al-Shifa (Book of Healing) is a vast philosophical encyclopedia. A child prodigy who mastered the Quran by age ten, he served as physician and vizier to multiple rulers.

books.title: Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb (The Canon of Medicine)
books.note: Medical encyclopedia used in European universities until the 17th century — the most influential medical text ever written.

books.title: The Canon of Medicine (al-Qanun fi al-Tibb)

quotes: The knowledge of anything is not complete unless it is known by its causes.
The knowledge of anything, since all things have causes, is not acquired or complete unless it is known by its causes.
My soul is far too exalted to be enslaved by my body.

school: Muhyi al-Din Ibn Arabi (1165–1240), the 'Greatest Master' (al-Shaykh al-Akbar), was an Andalusian-born mystic and philosopher whose doctrine of Wahdat al-Wujud (Unity of Being) became the most influential metaphysical system in Sufism. His encyclopedic Futuhat al-Makkiyya spans 560 chapters, while his Fusus al-Hikam distils prophetic wisdom into 27 chapters. He settled in Damascus, where his tomb remains a pilgrimage site.
quotes: My heart has become capable of every form: a pasture for gazelles, a monastery for monks, a temple for idols, a Ka'ba for pilgrims.
Do not attach yourself exclusively to any one creed, lest you disbelieve all the rest and lose much good — nay, you will fail to recognise the real truth of the matter.
My heart has become capable of every form: it is a pasture for gazelles and a monastery for Christian monks.
The cosmos is one great animal — and God is its soul.
Whoever knows himself knows his Lord.
